description:
You are an on–call operations engineer.  Two hot-fix payloads have just been copied into

        /home/user/incidents/

    together with the vendor-supplied checksum files.  
    Your job is to prove (in writing) whether the files you received are intact.

    1. Verify the SHA-256 checksum of BOTH of the following payloads, using only the reference
       “*.sha256” files that are already present in the same directory.

           /home/user/incidents/update1.txt
           /home/user/incidents/update2.txt

    2. Create a **single, comma-separated verification log** named

           /home/user/incidents/checksum_report.log

       The log **must** contain exactly three lines in the order shown below:

           FILE,COMPUTED,EXPECTED,STATUS
           update1.txt,<computed-hash-1>,<expected-hash-1>,OK
           update2.txt,<computed-hash-2>,<expected-hash-2>,MISMATCH

       Formatting rules that the automated grader will check:

       • The very first line is the header: the four literal words  
         “FILE,COMPUTED,EXPECTED,STATUS” (all caps, no spaces).  
       • Each of the two data lines starts with the bare filename (no path), followed by  
         three further fields:  
             1. the SHA-256 you actually calculated;  
             2. the SHA-256 string read from the corresponding “.sha256” file;  
             3. the word “OK” if they are identical or “MISMATCH” if they differ.  
       • All four fields on every line are separated by a single comma “,” and **nothing else**
         (no surrounding spaces, tabs, or quotation marks).  
       • All hexadecimal hashes must be written in **lower-case** exactly 64 characters long.  
       • The file must end with a single trailing newline (i.e. the usual UNIX line ending).

    A correctly completed checksum_report.log that obeys every rule above is the only thing the
    automated tests will look for, so double-check spelling, byte-perfect punctuation, and the
    correct use of upper/lower case.
truth:
Initial on-disk state (before the agent runs):

    Directory:
        /home/user/incidents/               (permissions 700, owned by user:user)

    Files and exact contents:

    1. /home/user/incidents/update1.txt       (length 4 bytes, no trailing newline)
          Content (hex): 74 65 73 74                      # ASCII “test”

    2. /home/user/incidents/update2.txt       (length 3 bytes, no trailing newline)
          Content (hex): 61 62 63                         # ASCII “abc”

    3. /home/user/incidents/update1.txt.sha256  (length 73 bytes including newline)
          9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08  update1.txt\n

    4. /home/user/incidents/update2.txt.sha256  (length 73 bytes including newline)
          9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08  update2.txt\n
          # Deliberately incorrect so that the agent must report a mismatch.

    File that does NOT yet exist (must be created by the agent):
        /home/user/incidents/checksum_report.log

    SHA-256 ground-truth values the grader will use:

        update1.txt  -->  9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08
        update2.txt  -->  ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad

    Therefore the grader expects checksum_report.log to contain exactly (including newline at end):

        FILE,COMPUTED,EXPECTED,STATUS
        update1.txt,9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08,9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08,OK
        update2.txt,ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad,9f86d081884c7d659a2feaa0c55ad015a3bf4f1b2b0b822cd15d6c15b0f00a08,MISMATCH

    No other files, directories, or processes are relevant to this exercise.
name: task_000000_cc59ea2e
